data.title: The Hidden Engine Behind Zeta's Surge
data.description:
Zeta Global Holdings (ZETA) is quickly finding itself to be one of the most differentiated companies in the enterprise marketing technology stack. Zeta’s latest offering, Athena, marks a pivotal point in Zeta’s trajectory as a company, but also in relation to how AI is going to shape enterprise operations in general.

In my earlier analysis, the focus was on Zeta Global’s evolving ability to drive deeper, more efficient monetization from its existing customer base, powered by multi-use adoption, expanding ARPU, and AI-driven workflow automation, which positions the company for a substantial valuation re-rating through 2028.

The focus now is how, following the latest earnings, Athena is driving a powerful wave of operational leverage and margin expansion that positions Zeta for a meaningful valuation re-rating as it increasingly behaves like an AI-native infrastructure company rather than a traditional martech platform.

The AI as the New UI Thesis

There is an emerging debate in Zeta’s contention that AI as the new UI expresses an even more fundamental philosophy of organizations, which is that the future of differentiation is in intelligent interfaces, not merely intelligent algorithms. Unlike most of its competitors, Zeta’s AI features have, from day one in 2017, been designed into the platform, which provides Athena an immense structural advantage, having an intelligence that rests upon a deterministic data engine as opposed to approximations of probability.

Athena is Zeta’s AI engine that automates workflows, analyzes data in real time, and delivers personalized recommendations to improve marketing performance. In practice, it acts like an intelligent assistant built directly into the platform, helping customers do more while reducing manual effort and cost.

Zeta’s Data Cloud, which is behind Athena, covers more than 242 million deterministic US individuals, in addition to over 550 million international profiles, updated in real time for billions of signals. This results in accurate personalization, accurate attribution, and compliant activation, which current general AI models find difficult to do.

The outcome is an AI solution that is able to root all recommendations in trustworthy, first-party data. For companies operating in a scenario of increasing constraints in privacy regulation, as well as the impending end of third-party cookies, this is a sustainable competitive barrier.

Athena’s interface is very effective at democratizing access to such intelligence. Zeta’s solution makes complex orchestration accessible to people who aren’t tech-savvy, which enables organizations to add more without having to add complexity at scale.

Operational Leverage and Margin Expansion

The playing field for operating leverage could be greatly impacted by Athena’s addition to this sector. Zeta’s adjusted EBITDA margin grew by 320 basis points from the previous year in Q3 of 2025 to 23.2%, which is also the company’s 19th consecutive period of improving margins, thanks to integration benefits from AI-powered automation, which cuts down human labor in campaign management, thus allowing Zeta to grow revenue without correspondingly increasing costs.

As Athena stimulates more Feature usage, the unit cost of revenue growth simultaneously decreases, fostering a compounding profitability trend. The Company’s free cash flow margin is already at 14%, an 83% year-over-year improvement, although a target of 70% of adjusted EBITDA to Free Cash Flow is set for 2030.

Longer-term, Athena is also a productivity amplifier for the organization as a whole. Through the automation of sales enablement, data modeling, and customer support, it has the potential to decrease selling and administrative costs expressed as a percentage of revenue. The compounding of this benefit could drive margins into the mid to high 20% range by 2027, which would be competitive with peer SaaS companies.

Competitive Landscape Outpacing Legacy AI Implementations

The race between AI in marketing technology is gaining pace, but this is because not all contenders have an equal level of ground to run from. The two current industry leaders in enterprise marketing, namely Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE), are essentially building upon an older infrastructure as they implement an additional layer of Generative AI Assistants in front of them.

The capabilities of Einstein Copilot by Salesforce, as well as Sensei GenAI from Adobe, in terms of content analysis as well as analytics, leave much to be desired, but they’re hobbled by infrastructure debt that’s several decades old, which impacts real-time capabilities.

Zeta, by contrast, is designed from scratch to incorporate AI integration, which makes it possible to natively integrate conversational intelligence capabilities during campaign execution. As for Zeta’s infrastructure, it supports native integration of conversational intelligence capabilities during campaign execution, which results in low latency, increased reliability, and closed-loop learning capabilities.

Furthermore, Zeta’s competitive advantage derives from focusing primarily on the mid-market and enterprise segment, without having to deal with the burden of implementing large projects in terms of costs. While an enterprise project in Salesforce could mean deploying in months or even years, costing millions of dollars, Zeta achieves an average deploy time of 50% sooner, along with an average ROI of 6:1, as shared during Zeta Live.

Valuation: A Re-Rating Story in Progress

Zeta’s valuation is becoming more dependent than ever upon the quality of its revenue runway, which provides a clear trajectory to significant scale. Analysts project revenue of $1.54 billion by 2026, and the guidance from management for 2028 of over $2.1 billion indicates strong revenue CAGR of an impressive 20%. At today’s levels, the stock trades at a forward P/S of roughly 3.2x for 2026 and just about 2x on 2028 estimates, multiples that appear unusually low for a company expanding this quickly while lifting margins.

The degree of visibility into Zeta’s future, combined with increasing margins and cash flow, makes current multiples appear increasingly disconnected from where the business is headed. As Zeta matures into a bigger, more optimized platform, it appears likely that so, too, will markets, creating significant upside as revenue compounds in the coming years.

Risks I Am Watching For

Even as Athena strengthens Zeta’s competitive advantages, certain risks to execution remain. The forthcoming combination of its enterprise software business, Marathon, into Marigold raises operational complexity inasmuch as product infrastructure and staff combine.

A slowdown in unlocking cross-sell benefits might slow growth’s trajectory for a period. There is also an adoptance risk because chat interfaces using AI remain in an early phase of an enterprise’s S-Curve, as well as Zeta’s potential exposure to payment cycles of an agency, which may lead to quarterly volatility in free cash flow even in strong fundamentals.

However, these risks appear to be more related to execution than structural problems, and having a strong balance sheet, stable revenue, as well as established margins, provides distinct protection against downside risk.

Final Take

Athena shows how Zeta Global is positioned as a rare AI-native platform that has real potential to reshape and redefine the infrastructure of enterprise marketing. As it refines the usage experience from code to conversation, it does more than merely drive customer engagement but also forges ways to tap into revenue streams.

As adoption continues to expand, and the market begins to understand compounding economics, Zeta’s valuation discount appears increasingly unsustainable. For those investors looking for differentiated exposure to the nexus of AI, Data, and Enterprise SaaS, Athena may prove to be what catalyzes Zeta’s potential into actualized market dominance.

data.title: RAPT Therapeutics: Strong Promise In Immunology, Looking Like An Opportunity At These Prices
data.description:
Topline Summary

RAPT Therapeutics (RAPT) is a company I've taken one look at before, several years ago, coming to the conclusion that this immunology-focused biotech had generated market value far, far beyond their data at the time. Since then, the share price has fallen over 80% (although the total valuation of the company has dropped more modestly, from around $850 million in 2023 to more like $450 million at the time of writing). So the company has absolutely come down to earth, and they've had much more time to mature their pipeline. Today, I want to make a case for a tentative "Buy" thesis, and one that could pay off if you navigate the risks carefully.

Pipeline Overview - Immunology Focus With Broad Implications

RAPT has built its pipeline on two prongs: agents that block immunoglobulin E (IgE), and agents that block CCR4, both of which are implicated in inflammation, making it a potentially suitable approach in rheumatology and cancer therapy.

Ozurpreprubart (RPT904)

The most advanced candidate in the RAPT pipeline is ozurpreprubart, an antibody blocking IgE. This immunoglobulin is a principal player in inflammatory reactions generated by allergens like peanuts. Ozurpreprubart is currently in mid-stage clinical study for a few indications, most notably chronic spontaneous urticaria (aka hives).

This indication was the subject of a few key catalysts for RAPT in recent months. Back in October, the company announced a positive topline data readout for ozurpreprubart from a phase 2 trial. The study took 137 patients with urticaria not well controlled with antihistamines and randomized them to either ozurpreprubart or the approved agent omalizumab. Patients receiving ozurpreprubart had a numeric improvement in the urticaria activity score over omalizumab, and this improvement (or at least no clear sign of decrement) was achieved with dosing every 8 weeks or every 12 weeks, compared with the every-4-week dosing of omalizumab.

These findings point to ozurpreprubart being a more favorable option, given the longer spans between doses and favorable tolerability. RAPT and their Chinese partner anticipate advancing this compound to a phase 3 trial, although the exact timeline for this study to begin has not been shared.

Another important area being explored for ozurpreprubart is food allergy. RAPT has recently initiated a randomized, phase 2 trial evaluating the agent compared with placebo in ~100 people with at least one food allergy, with a primary endpoint of a prespecified target improvement in a controlled, oral food challenge.

Tivumecirnon

Tivumecirnon is an oral molecule blocking CCR4, opening potential avenues for T-cell lymphoma and other forms of cancer. This agent was actually one of the main areas of focus in my first write-up on the company a few years ago. However, not much other progress has been completed in favor of pursuing ozurpreprubart with all force. As such, to date only a pair of early trials have been completed, and the agent no longer features prominently in their corporate presentation. So for now it would appear as though tivumecirnon is on the back burner.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, RAPT held $37.9 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $119.4 million in marketable securities. Operating expenses were $19.4 million, and after other income, the net loss for the quarter was $17.6 million. For the first 9 months of 2025, the cash burn was $76.1 million. At this rate, RAPT has a cash runway of approximately 6 quarters. However, these figures do not take into account proceeds from a public offering of common stock that RAPT expects to bring in a gross of $250 million.

This could add several more years of operational runway for RAPT to continue their pivotal trial developments, bringing the cash runway to around 16 quarters, with the assumption that costs remain the same.

Strengths and Risks

After years of work, RAPT appears poised to generate pivotal data that could lead to approval in urticaria, a space where Xolair (omalizumab mentioned above) currently commands global sales in the multiple billions. But there are some issues to consider.

Strength - Favorable randomized phase 2 data

While their trial was designed neither as a superiority nor a non-inferiority study, ozurpreprubart has shown a strong signal that it could at least replace Xolair with less frequent dosing. The randomized, phase 3 trial could end up showing that ozurpreprubart is, in fact, better than omalizumab, with the potential to capture a large portion of the market.

Risk - Not the Only Game in town

There are several treatment approaches to chronic spontaneous urticaria. The most recent of these is remibrutinib, an oral inhibitor of BTK approved in patients who are symptomatic despite treatment with antihistamines. And that drug passed the phase 3 bar in two separate trials published in the New England Journal. This ignores that omalizumab and dupilumab are both available, as well, and have been for years.

All of this is to say that ozurpreprubart faces competition even if it does get approval, and we do not yet know that they will be able to differentiate this compound from competitors adequately. In particular, patients may elect for the BTK inhibitor more often, since it is orally dosed.

Risk - Cash burn will accelerate and may place pressure on RAPT at an inconvenient time

Over the years, RAPT has remained fairly resilient in total market valuation despite dilution that has led to a steep decline in share price. This latest round of common stock offering shows what a developmental biotech can do from a position of strength. An informal calculation suggests they now have something like 16 quarters of cash to continue their projects (taking into account their recent public offering), but it is important to keep in mind that a large, phase 3 trial will incur extra cash burn, and new forays into food allergy will also elevate the operational outlay.

If they're unable to continue keeping costs under control, then RAPT may find themselves in need of another dilutive financing before their phase 3 trial reads out.

Bottom-Line Summary

RAPT has definitely come a long way since my first outing with the company. And I have very much evolved my position in favor of the thesis, especially now that the company is trading at only a small premium relative to their cash after they complete their public offering.

So to me it is clearly no longer a "Sell" for me, but the question is whether these data are strong enough to justify a "Buy." And given the market size, I think ozurpreprubart has the potential to demonstrate a niche, and the phase 3 trial may well end up establishing this agent as a standard of care.

So for now I am upgrading my sentiment to "Buy," given the promise of their pipeline, the market size they're targeting (with currently approved drugs commanding billions in global sales per year), and the strong signals of efficacy relative to omalizumab. In particular, the fact that the market now values this pipeline in the tens of millions of dollars suggests that these price levels represent a viable entry point. I think RAPT is definitely worth watching closely for further development, or potentially a small position for people willing to take the risk of a phase 3 trial that will take a while to develop.

data.title: The 3% World: The Bond Market Tells What The FED Won't Say (But Markets Don't Care)
data.description:
A New "Tolerated" Regime

The world has changed.

Inflation, unemployment rates, Treasury yields, and interest rates are all in different ranges from where we were used to seeing them. We initially thought that this could be the long-tail effect of the pandemic. By now, we can probably argue that the macroeconomic paradigm has changed.

In this article, I want to explain what is happening, showing the impact that it can have on our investing strategies and the way we build our portfolios.

Macroeconomic Reasons That Make 2% Inflation Impossible

The ZIRP era is behind us, and inflation won't be at 2% for some time. There are structural cost pressures that can't be simply wiped away. The first one is linked to tariffs. The Yale Budget Lab, for example, calculates that in the short run, tariffs imply a 1.7% increase in consumer prices. But even without these levies, we live in a world where wage growth is still stronger than before the pandemic. Zero Interest Rate Policy belongs, as of now, to economic history handbooks and describes the period from 2009 to 2021. That's when money was basically free, and FED fund rates were mainly between 0% and 0.25%. Those who signed a mortgage back then are now feasting and have no hurry whatsoever in paying it down. Inflation is melting the value of their debt down, while wages are increasing at 3.6%.

One of the drivers of this increase in labor cost is the aging workforce, which naturally shifts workers toward higher-paying jobs. Participation in the labor market is also below pre-pandemic levels. Supply chain constraints keep appearing, creating both labor scarcity and harder-to-achieve productivity gains. Simply put, the cost structure is overall increasing. We then need to consider that China is not viewed anymore as the cheap factory of the world and that the U.S. is pursuing a re-industrialization policy focused on reshoring or nearshoring most of the manufacturing capacity it needs. This implies higher input costs.

Third, massive infrastructure spending driven by the AI buildout is pouring billions into the economy, which is, per se, an inflationary force at work (the article "Capex Nation" that I wrote deals with this in depth).

Therefore, inflation is now precisely at 3%. Even though we know that the FED's mandate targets 2% inflation over the long term, many facts signal that the FED is probably accepting 3% inflation for some time. It has not been explicitly said, but its behavior suggests that 3% inflation can be tolerated, at least as long as it doesn't accelerate. After all, accepting 3% right now still means that YoY inflation has cooled. In fact, if the FED really wanted inflation to be at 2% quickly, it could have tightened its rate policy this summer, when inflation trended upward from 2.8% to 3.1%. Instead, it first paused hikes and then cut rates, also signaling that, perhaps, more cuts could be down the line. The economic cost of pushing inflation rapidly to 2% is high; it would imply more unemployment and lower wage growth. But, right now, with inflation somewhat tamed, though at a higher level than the FED's mandate targets, the FED has pivoted to prioritizing the labor market since the unemployment rate has reached 4.5%.

As a consequence, Treasuries keep yielding above 4%, and this will last longer than expected. I think this is also an indicator that signals that investors are expecting 3% inflation for some time. The result is that the curve of Treasury yields has to shift higher. Yields don't know anything about the FED's mandate; they simply reflect what investors want as real returns. With inflation at 3%, a 4% risk-free return is the minimum standard that is required, calculated as the sum of expected inflation and the premium investors want. The bond market has been trading at these levels since 2022, with only a short period of time between 2022 and 2023 where yields dropped to around 3.5%. High inflation could help the government reduce the real value of its debt. But, at the same time, it creates a situation where it has to pay back this advantage in the form of higher interest rates. So, the trade-off is a short-run real debt erosion in exchange for paying higher interest in the long run.

What does this mean? Well, a 3% world is—structurally - structurally speaking - a higher-yield world.

Consequences In A 3% World

If we use the BLS historical CPI from 1913 to 2024, we see that the average inflation in the 80s was 5.1% and that in the 90s it was around 3%. Then, in the first decade of this century, we see it move down to 2.6% and in the 10s it averaged 1.8%. So, inflation had been falling for over 40 years until 2021. We don't know if history will repeat, but it could take up to a decade or even fifteen years for the average inflation to be clearly down below 2%. Right now, this decade has averaged around 4%.

Now, let's combine this with the average 10-year Treasury yield. Let's look at what happened in the past 5 decades. In the 80s, the average yield was above 10%. In the 90s, it retraced to 6.7%. In the early 2000s, the 10-yr average yield was 4.5%. Then, in the 10s, the yield dropped to 2.4$. At the end of the ZIRP era, it was even down to a minuscule 1.2%. Since the post-pandemic rebound, the yield has moved up quite a bit and is now between 3.8% and 4.5%. For more than three consecutive years, the 10-year Treasury yield has consistently been above 3.5%. So, the downtrend is broken, and the yield curve is showing a structural change that reflects a true repricing. Where is the underlying fundamental reason? Inflation. Or more precisely, the premium over inflation that investors demand.

Now let's combine these data with what the New York Fed points out about the real way yields are formed:

In standard economic theory, yields on Treasury securities are composed of two components: expectations of the future path of short-term Treasury yields and the Treasury term premium. The term premium is defined as the compensation that investors require for bearing the risk that interest rates may change over the life of the bond.

In the 2010s, expectations of future rates were close to 0 while the term premium was also kept very low by QE and extremely low inflation. Today, the market expects higher short-term rates, while the term premium is once again building up. The market simply wants to be more protected against higher inflation (or, at least, against inflation risk) and rate volatility.

This chart below shows exactly how the 10-year Treasury yield is made up of two components: in blue we see the 10-year yield; in red we see the term premium, while the yellow line represents the fitted yield, that is, what the yield should be when we add short-term expectations and the term premium. Starting from the 1980s, we see three important things:

  1. The downtrend from the early 80s to 2020 (from 15% to just 1%). It shows what many have called the long bond bull market.
  2. The term premium fell from 3-4% into negative territory, where it stayed from 2015 until 2021. This means that investors were ok to accept yields below the expected short-rate yield for holding long-term bonds. This was possible only because inflation was extremely low, and QE de facto removed duration from the market.
  3. After 2021, the regime changed, with higher 10-year yields and a positive term premium, showing that both components of how Treasury yields are formed have moved and are now higher.

Now, interest rates averaged -0.05% from 2011 to 2020. Now, we are seeing rates with positive real yields. This also represents a shift, or, as some believe, simply returning to normality. With inflation allowed to stay around 3%, the FED has more room between the neutral stance (interest rates around 3%) and 0%. Which means the so-called dovish area widens. But the chart above shows that the 10-year yield is not higher now just because interest rates are higher, but also because investors demand higher compensation due to a more uncertain outlook.

Investing Implications

First of all, long-duration bonds should now be underweighted. Short-term Treasuries have a better risk/reward ratio in an environment where inflation is stickier than expected, and at 3%.

Secondly, and perhaps the most important thing to say, discount rates now have to be higher, which leads to lower multiples. Equity risk premia should rise, and, logically speaking, we should expect that market multiples in a 3% inflation regime should be lower compared to those of the last decade.

Third, what kinds of businesses thrive in this environment? Well, companies that can pass through inflation rather than suffer extreme pressure on their margins will probably fare better than many others. Consumer discretionary stocks, but also levered REITS, could be some of the losers, while commodity and energy plays could act as natural hedges.

This leads us to a paradox, or at least a contradiction, in today's market. The macro case we have built shows that structurally higher inflation, rates, yields, and term premia lead to lower valuation multiples. But the market right now is doing exactly the opposite, since valuations are expanding, especially when we consider AI-related names. Is this a bubble? Well, not really. In fact, while many companies (look at the hyperscalers) do trade at higher multiples compared to the 2010s, their earnings growth is, generally speaking, accelerating or, at least, maintaining a brisk pace. This happens even if the cost of capital is now higher.

The AI capex cycle, as we said earlier, is inflationary. For example, where data centers are built, electricity prices surge. But AI capex is an investment that is also producing earnings growth. When companies grow faster than their cost of capital, the market usually bids their prices up. So, multiples right now are supported by sustained earnings growth. Actually, we could even say that AI is so big that its effect currently dominates over the other structural trends that should lead to multiple compressions. It also explains why the market is currently pushed higher by a handful of companies, while most of the companies in the S&P 500 are not compounding as fast.

We often hear people saying that the market is only fueled by the Mag 7 and that the S&P 493 is hardly doing anything. Well, perhaps we now see why. The market should see multiple compressions right now. But a big megatrend has started that is producing earnings growth for some companies, especially the hyperscalers and the few other companies that are positioned at key bottlenecks of the supply chain. Right now, the Mag-7 represents almost 36% of the index. In 2023, the market rose 26.3%, with the Mag 7 contributing with 15.8%, an extremely large chunk of the market's total returns. In 2024, the market gained another 25.7%, with the Mag 7 contributing with 14.1%. So far, the market is up 16% and the Mag 7 account for over half of these returns. Perhaps the market is not behaving in an irrational way, but we have a hard time identifying the correct way to interpret what is happening.

Conclusion

Indeed, the landscape has changed, and we can't just expect to go back to the 2010s. After all, even as investors, we are probably sitting on huge gains with our portfolios at ATHs. I mean, we can be well-off, even in this macroeconomic environment. Inflation seems to be settled around 3% and the bond market is seeing a structural repricing process. In this context, long-duration assets lose steam because discount rates go naturally higher, lowering valuation multiples. But, at the same time, the AI capex boom is reshaping the market, producing strong earnings growth for a selected number of companies, whose strong performance is so meaningful that it is able to drive up the whole market, pushing the S&P 500 to richer multiples, even if Treasuries yield 4%.

The playbook for investors seems to me clear. Shorter maturities will be favored. We will use higher discount rates in our valuation models, and we will favor companies with strong pricing power (ever heard of Ferrari?), high ROIC, or direct and immediately understandable exposure to AI. Sectors that benefit from inflation will also be rich in opportunities, while others will probably suffer greatly from higher inflation.

A 3% world is not a low-rate paradise, but it is also not a bad world for investors. We simply have to pick stocks having in mind a new mindset.

data.title: Royalty Pharma: Guidance Raised Again, Platform Strength Intact -- Buy Reiterated
data.description:
Following Royalty Pharma plc's (NASDAQ:RPRX) Investor Day (Fig. 1), we are returning with an update in light of the company's supportive Q3 results and its latest investment. Here at the Lab, we reiterate a Buy rating on the back of solid execution and a fresh upward revision to guidance. The company still trades at a notable discount to biopharma peers, despite 86% of the portfolio being tied to approved therapies and free cash flow expanding at a healthy clip. With the Investor Day, Royalty Pharma management targets 11–14% portfolio receipts CAGR through 2030, and continues to deploy capital with discipline, including selective M&A.

Fig 1

Q3 Results and Our Positive Stance

In the quarter, RP's portfolio receipts grew by 11% to $814 million (Fig. 2), exceeding the average consensus expectation of $765 million. The portfolio increased mainly thanks to Voranigo, Tremfya, and the cystic fibrosis franchise. On the cash flow statement, the company reported operating activities of $703 million. For Q3, RP ROIC and ROE reached 15.7% and 22.9%, respectively (Fig. 3). Regarding capital allocation, RP deployed approximately $1 billion in the quarter, driven by upfront payments under the Imdelltra and Obexelimab funding agreements. YTD, the deployment reached $1.7 billion. At the same time, aside from a dividend payment, the company has repurchased approximately 4 million shares in Q3, and $1.2 billion in 9M. Once again, RP raised its full-year 2025 outlook with "Portfolio Receipts expected to be $3,200 to $3,250 million."

Source: Royalty Pharma Q3 results - Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we still positive?

Even after a strong +55% YTD performance, the company still sits about 20% below the following days' IPO offerings, underscoring fragile market sentiment. Here at the Lab, we have a contrarian view here. This is backed by the fact that the company raised the guidance three times this year, Q1 (May 8) - Q2 (Aug 6), and following the Q3 results. Additionally, this new guidance still factors in conservative assumptions regarding cystic fibrosis franchise royalties

More importantly, the biotech market is still heavily disrupted. The company offers generalist investors a diversified route into biopharma without the single-drug binary risk typical of the sector. Here at the Lab, we believe the market has not fully understood RP's potential. This is a company that functions as a platform-style aggregator. The key to the report is the fact that pharma R&D costs are rising and capital needs are expanding. The company's business is royalty financing, and it is gaining traction as a non-dilutive complement to equity and debt. Additionally, the company commands roughly half of the royalty market, reinforcing its central role as the asset class and its firepower to pursue bigger deals.

There is a strong asset-quality mix. Its portfolio focuses on royalties from scale franchises with high commercial potential therapies. However, the company recently announced a royalty financing deal for Alnylam’s Amvuttra (Fig. 4). The company projects nominal peak sales for Amvuttra of almost $11 billion at a 1% royalty rate. Having said that, there is also pipeline portfolio progress ahead, which includes six Ph3 readouts over the next year. We see these near-term readouts supporting 12 drug launches over the next three years, contributing to earnings growth and mid-term diversification.

Fig 4

Valuation

During the investor day, we already moved the RP valuation closer to our 2026 estimates. Today, we make no change to our numbers. We still imply a portfolio receipts growth of 9% (while the company aims to achieve 11-14% growth). On the EPS, we see a correct forecast of 10% growth (while the company implied an annual EPS growth rate of approximately 12% until 2030). This is very much supported by RP Management incorporation and a lower cost structure. Therefore, our 2026 net profit is set at €2.8 billion.

Compared to last time, the company repurchased shares and now includes 577.24 million shares outstanding. So our EPS reached $4.85.

With no change to our valuation methodology, we applied a 10x P/E target, increasing our valuation from $47 to $48.5 per share. This still implied a 35% discount vs its reference pharma sector. The company's target P/E is also aligned with its five-year historical average. The business fundamentals remain strong, and we see no reason for the sector discount. Larger transactions and earnings diversification are likely to sustain RP stock market outperformance in the near term.

Risks

RP downside risks include the Vertex franchise development and lower portfolio receipts growth (with worse-than-expected performance of current investments). This is due to increased competition and the entry of incumbent players into the market. The company is subject to interest rate risk given its debt; however, if rates rise, it is well-positioned for a better deal environment. Other risks include lower synergies from integrating the RP Management team and from value-destroying deals.

Conclusion

The Investor Day provided a tailwind for RP; however, the company's strong execution and track record are proving that it has an extended and reliable plan. With a double-digit ROE, an ongoing buyback, and many catalysts ahead, we remain buyers.

data.title: Aura Biosciences: Bringing Not-Quite-Viruses Closer To Fruition In Cancer
data.description:
Topline Summary

It has been several years since I took a first look at Aura Biosciences (AURA) and their unique light-activated virus-like treatment approach. I found the data they had generated at that time to be "tantalizing," and, obviously, a cool technology. But it was not enough to justify the company's lofty valuation, relative to their holdings.

Today, after a long absence from the stock, I want to take a closer look at this thesis, taking into account the many leaps forward they've made in the pipeline. However, despite the progress, my enthusiasm here is subdued, upgrading the sentiment more in favor of a watch-and-wait approach. The risks just barely outweigh the potential gain at these price levels, in my opinion, and I hope to make clear in this article why I think that remains so.

Pipeline Overview - Leveraging Unique Properties of Local Therapies for the Eye and Bladder

Belzupacap sarotalocan (bel-sar)

The lone candidate therapy in the AURA pipeline remains bel-sar, a virus-like particle that binds heparan sulfate to be internalized into cancer cells, where they release a particle designed to sensitize the cell to near-infrared light. This makes it a particularly intriguing localized therapy for malignancies that impact the eye, like uveal melanoma.

Indeed, uveal is the main shot on goal for bel-sar at this time, and an ongoing phase 3 trial called CoMpass is evaluating the use of bel-sar in patients with early choroidal melanoma compared with a sham procedure. The initiation of this trial is supported by findings from a phase 2 trial finalized last year, which enrolled 22 patients with early choroidal melanoma.

Among 10 patients who received doses of bel-sar that would be relevant in the phase 3 trial, 9 had vision preservation according to a change in BCVA. Eight of 10 of these patients had "tumor control" at 12 months, and patients who received at least the chosen phase 3 dose appeared to have better progression-free survival than did those who received "subtherapeutic" doses, implying a dose-response relationship and supporting the hypothesis that bel-sar is making an impact on tumor growth.

In their Q2 update, AURA disclosed that 240 patients had been enrolled into a screening run-in phase of the study, with the intention of identifying patients who have documented growth of their tumor. This increased to 280 in the Q3 update, and AURA stated that they had activated approximately 90% of the target clinical sites. They expect that enrollment of the 100 patients eligible for the trial will be complete in 2026 and that they anticipate a top-line readout in Q4 2027.

Other eye-related projects include a phase 2 trial evaluating bel-sar in patients who have metastases to the choroid from other cancers. Initial data from this trial are anticipated later this year. Another phase 1 trial is evaluating bel-sar in cancers on the surface of the eye. Both of these trials have a readout anticipated in 2026.

Bel-sar is also being explored as a treatment option for bladder cancer, based on the findings from a small window-of-opportunity trial suggesting antitumor activity that was presented earlier this year. An ongoing phase 1b/2 trial is enrolling approximately 26 patients with intermediate- and high-risk non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, either in lieu of a resection or immediately prior to one.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, AURA held $47.6 million in cash and equivalents and $114.3 million in marketable securities. Operating expenses were $27.9 million, and after interest, the net loss for the quarter was $26.1 million. And in the first 9 months of 2025, AURA used $64.3 million in cash to fund operations. At this burn rate, they can sustain operations for between 7 and 8 quarters. This remains consistent with management guidance that cash should last "into the first half of 2027."

Strengths and Risks

AURA has come a long way since I took a first look at them more than 2 years ago, and while there are no imminent concerns with the stock, here are the main factors that I am considering at this time for the investment thesis.

Strength - Interesting early signals in an area without competition

For these forms of ocular melanoma, patients who need some form of treatment face the real possibility of a surgery with dramatic impact on their sight. Treatment approaches that can prevent the progression of the disease without needing to resort to a more radical treatment would be welcome, and AURA has shown some early signs that they can be just that. And being in the middle of a phase 3 trial puts the goalpost within sight, in theory.

Mixed - Cash position is strong for now, but insufficient to get to a seminal signal

I have no reason to disagree with the management guided cash runway of getting into 2027, but unfortunately their own guidance says that we won't have a topline readout of the phase 3 trial in early 2027. To me, it's a signal that AURA is going to be looking for solutions to a cash challenge by late next year, but with no clear catalysts that could propel them to a position of strength that might enable a definitive cash raise.

Risk - Still very much unproven, despite early signals

It is definitely important to keep in mind that this is a tech that remains to be seen as a viable entity in the management of ocular melanoma. We've only seen relevant data from 10 patients who have received a phase 3 dose, and while the visual control was interesting, it is far from a definitive signal.

The ongoing phase 3 trial is going to be critical to establish the real proof, and AURA has disclosed challenges in recruitment over the course of 2025 due to the need to document bona fide tumor growth in choroidal melanoma. Their new guidance of a topline readout in late 2027 is now clear, but it's still quite a long time for the market to wait.

Bottom-Line Summary

In my first outing on AURA, I had severe reservations about the long lag time until they might be able to show any kind of clinical signal, let alone one that would be strong enough to get really excited about a novel treatment modality like bel-sar. And the valuation of the company at the time I felt was unsustainable.

At this point, I am still inclined to believe that AURA is overvalued, but with much less conviction. They have reached a point where we've seen some proof of concept, and the company is running a phase 3 trial that could very well be the key to approval, and for a lot of companies that alone would be enough to justify a "buy." But this is a trial that is still something like 2 years away from reading out, and it is the current best shot at a first approval. It's pretty hard to ignore them outright at this point, but I also can't really justify a "buy" sentiment.

I am upgrading my sentiment from "Strong Sell" to a "Hold." There are interesting things to like about this one, but I would not dive in just yet, at least not until we have a clearer timeline for the CoMpass trial.

data.title: Buy The Dip: I'm Betting Big On These Dirt-Cheap Income Machines
data.description:
While the overall stock market has been on a strong bull run since the correction in the immediate wake of President Trump's Liberation Day tariff announcement, not all stocks have soared. In fact, there are numerous dividend-paying stocks that have pulled back sharply recently. In particular, there are several that have strong underlying fundamentals and have attractive growth outlooks in front of them, yet have pulled back sharply recently, thereby presenting investors with a great opportunity to buy these quality businesses at deep discounts and lock in dividend yields that are superior to many of their peers in their respective sectors.

In this article, I am going to detail two of these dividend machines that I am making big bets on right now.

Big Bet #1

The first one I am going to discuss is B2Gold (BTG). BTG is a gold mining stock that has a diversified portfolio, with most of its production coming from Mali, but it also has a recently launched mine in Canada (Goose) that helps to further diversify its operations away from geopolitically risky Mali. I loaded up on it earlier this year and sold it in the late summer to lock in very attractive gains and recycle the capital into gold bullion leases in search of a better yield and lower downside risk.

Since then, the stock has sold off sharply due to the company providing disappointing near-term Goose production guidance and troublesome headlines from Mali, despite the company continuing with business as usual in the country and continuing to expect strong long-term production from its Goose mine.

As such, it today trades at a steep discount on a price-to-NAV basis (35%) relative to its own history, as it has historically traded in line with its net asset value. Additionally, it trades at an even larger discount to many of its peers like Newmont (NEM), Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), and even Barrick Mining (B). It also now offers an attractive dividend yield of 1.9%, which is well above that offered by many of its peers and the overall gold (GLD) mining sector.

Moreover, it has a very attractive free cash flow yield on a next 12-month basis based on analyst consensus estimates of 14.16%, which is very high compared to its historic average of 4.19%, and analysts expect its earnings per share to grow at a very impressive 18.1% CAGR through the end of the decade, making it a highly compelling growth play while still paying out an attractive current yield and also having the potential for significant valuation multiple expansion.

Additionally, the company delivered strong production in its Fekola, Masbate, and Otjikoto mines, while Goose began to contribute some of its initial ounces as well. The company is also benefiting significantly from strong gold prices, which are driving its earnings per share and free cash flow materially higher. The company recently reiterated its 2025 production guidance and continues to generate very strong profits thanks to the elevated gold price. The one big recent hiccup was that the Goose mine, which recently came online, is suffering from a crushing bottleneck and also delayed access to underground, which has caused the company to cut production guidance in the mine for this year. However, 2026 and 2027 production targets have been unchanged, and the CAPEX at the mine is largely behind it, so the long-term intrinsic value of the asset is relatively unimpaired.

Meanwhile, management emphasized that operations in Mali remained unaffected despite the negative headlines of al-Qaeda militias causing chaos and other miners' permits being negatively impacted. BTG also continues to have all permits in good standing, and their operations remain unimpacted. Additionally, both Fekola and Otjikoto continue to generate significant cash flow for the company while also being able to advance organic growth and cost improvement operations.

The company's balance sheet also remains in solid shape, with about one-third of their prepaid ounces due already delivered. The company even repurchased some shares during the most recent quarter to emphasize their strong cash flow generation, solid balance sheet position, and the attractive valuation of the stock. Additionally, once Goose reaches a steady state and Antelope is funded internally, management could very possibly turn to increasing the buyback and or the dividend.

All told, 2026 and beyond continue to look very attractive for BTG, with the main risk being things in Mali deteriorating further. However, it comes with the territory, and thus far BTG has navigated that very well. Additionally, there are other mining stocks that operate in risky jurisdictions, yet they are not in the penalty box nearly to the degree that BTG is, so it seems that the market's main concern is the recent hiccup at Goose. However, these issues are being resolved and should not weigh on the company over the long term. Therefore, I believe that while BTG is a high-risk bet, I think the total return upside is so significant that it is a very attractive bet to make right now. As a result, I recently bought back into the stock.

Big Bet #2

The other income machine that I am betting big on right now is Blue Owl Capital (OWL). It is an alternative asset manager spread across the private credit (OBDC)(OTF), GP stakes, and real asset spaces. Its real asset business includes a digital infrastructure business that is growing rapidly and is expected to benefit from the ongoing AI build-out in the coming years. It has already secured major deals with Meta (META) and OpenAI (OPENAI) to partner with them in building and ultimately leasing to them data centers. It is also a major player in Project Stargate, thereby positioning it to enjoy significant growth with attractive investment products for its institutional clients for years to come.

These tailwinds are expected to drive very strong assets under management growth with a fairly low-risk, attractive investment profile that should lead to very stable returns for both its clients and attractive growth for OWL shareholders. In addition, it remains a strong player in the private credit space and GP stakes business as well as its triple net lease real estate business, all of which position it to grow at a strong clip for years to come.

Management, at its investor day earlier this year, guided for a 20% plus earnings per share growth and reiterated that it remains on track for its growth targets at its most recent earnings call. While analysts are a little bit less bullish, they still forecast around a 16% earnings per share CAGR through the end of the decade.

When coupled with its 6.4% dividend yield, strong investment-grade balance sheet, significant amount of permanent capital, and discounted valuation relative to peers like Blackstone (BX) and Ares (ARES), which trade at materially higher price-to-earnings ratios than OWL while also having significantly lower dividend yields, OWL looks like a highly compelling opportunity to bet on.

Yes, there are risks such as negative headlines impacting the private credit space and concerns about overblown AI hype that will lead to a crash in the AI bubble. However, it is important to keep in mind that OWL's private credit portfolio has performed very well for quite some time with a very low-loss rate. And today it remains conservatively positioned, with investments almost entirely in first-lien senior secured loans.

Meanwhile, its data center business is a much more defensive play on AI as these are long-duration leases with protection clauses for them as the landlord relative to some of the highest-quality companies in the world as the counterparties. Therefore, if these leases go belly up, it means that leading mega-cap stocks are likely crashing very hard. Therefore, the downside risk here is more in the nature of the growth of the business underperforming rather than the actual assets under management declining sharply. That risk is certainly more than priced into the stock at current levels, making it an attractive risk-adjusted bet as part of a diversified portfolio.

Investor Takeaway

Between BTG and OWL, investors can lean into some major macro trends like AI, private credit, and soaring gold prices at a steep discount while also locking in attractive dividend yields. As a result, these are two of the biggest bets I am making, along with several other similar ones in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: SMCY: High Risk, Low Reward
data.description:
Thesis

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) has been a market darling in the past few years, posting some eye-watering returns:

  • 2021: 38%
  • 2022: 84%
  • 2023: 242%

It was only expected that after such a performance we would see a single-name ETF here, and we did. YieldMax came up with the YieldMax SMCI Option Income Strategy ETF (SMCY) in 2024, which, as per its objective:

Investors gain exposure to Super Micro Computer Inc, while also seeking current income. The fund is designed to provide investors with the potential opportunity for consistent weekly income through a covered call option strategy.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at SMCY and its structure, and articulate why this name represents a high risk, low reward fund.

Fund composition - a synthetic long

Let us look at what the fund owns and what it does:

There are two major holdings here:

  1. Cash and cash equivalents: we can see the name holding T-bills and a money market fund.
  2. Options positions.

The options positions represent a synthetic long for SMCI, layered with call options to extract dividends. What do we mean? Let us take a look at the row highlighted in blue above. The row represents a short position in a Jan 16, 2026, put option on SMCI with a $44/share strike. SMCI is currently trading at $37/share, which means the fund wrote a put on a much higher strike. That put comes with a 0.72 delta, and the fund being short the put translates into a long position for the stock (i.e., if the stock does not move, the fund will be assigned the stock at $44/share come January 16, 2026). As a reminder, a simple long position in SMCI equity has a delta of 1. The fund is also long a call at the same strike and maturity, thus creating a synthetic long position:

A synthetic long asset is a strategy for options trading that is designed to mimic a long stock position. Traders create a synthetic long asset by purchasing at-the-money (ATM) calls and then selling an equivalent number of ATM puts with the same date of expiration.

We are not going to bore readers with complex finance lingo here, but the point is that the ETF is employing complex financial engineering rather than the simple approach of owning the stock and writing 2-week covered calls (or 1-month calls, or a ladder). The takeaway from this section is that an unnecessarily complex strategy is employed here. Let us see if the strategy works.

The performance of the strategy is very, very poor

Let us go back to basics. You are an investor who does not know anything about finance, but you like the stock. You therefore bought SMCI in November 2024 (a year ago), and you are holding it. Let us see how you did versus paying YieldMax 0.99% to employ a complex synthetic long strategy:

A simple buy-and-hold purchase would have produced a 74% total return, while SMCY produced a 25% total return. Please note we are using a total return feature here to account for the large dividends, because from a pure price perspective, SMCY is down -58% in the past year.

Any single-name ETF that employs options represents a good choice as long as its total return is within a certain band versus holding the stock outright. Kindly keep in mind that SMCY does not employ options to generate alpha (i.e., take views on SMCI), but uses options to extract dividends. It is just a fancy way of replacing a buy-and-sell strategy. When the basis is larger than 20% in terms of returns, we no longer believe such an ETF serves its purpose.

In our example above, buying $100 worth of SMCI a year ago would give you $174 in your brokerage account, while buying SMCY would give you a net position of $125. All while the downside risk is the same.

Same risk on the downside

After touching $60/share in early October 2025, the SMCI is down significantly in the past month:

If we compare SMCI with SMCY using total returns, we can see the downside is almost identical. There is a small buffer there, but it is fairly reduced. When SMCI tanks, SMCY follows. Which explains the massive risk figures you see for SMCY on the Seeking Alpha risk tab:

  • Standard deviation: 48%
  • Volatility: 74%

So SMCY has virtually all the downside risk, but only recorded one third of the upside in the past year, thus making it a high-risk, low reward type of investment.

How to think about single-name ETFs

The framework for single-name ETFs should always revolve around graphing a total return. As an investor, when buying a single-name ETF, you are basically outsourcing the capital gains harvesting to the extent the instrument is structured properly. In order to check that the instrument is a solid investment, you have to run a total return graph that benchmarks the single-name ETF versus the outright stock. If the difference (i.e., basis) is larger than 20%, then you should just move away from such a fund because it does not fulfill its goal. Such a fund will always leave you with all the downside, while lagging significantly in an up-market.

Conclusion

SMCI has been a market darling in the past, thus prompting the creation of a single name ETF in SMCY. The single-name ETF comes with a high 0.99% expense ratio and all the downside of owning SMCI. The upside, however, is not tracked very well, with SMCY up only 25% in the past year versus 74% for SMCI outright. We do not think the structuring here is good enough, and there is no reason for an investor to hold this single-name ETF in its current format. Just buy SMCI outright and sell portions of it as it appreciates in order to create your own high yield while capturing the entire upside.

data.title: Senstar Technologies: Downside Risk Looks Limited In Security Solutions Provider
data.description:
Intro

Our initial coverage related to Senstar Technologies Corporation (SNT) was in December 2020, when the company traded as the larger 'Magal Security Systems' entity. However, post the sale of Magal's projects division to Aeronautics Ltd. in 2023, the remaining company was rebranded 'Senstar Technologies,' which trades under the ticker 'SNT' today. Therefore, Senstar now operates in the security and protection industry as a core international supplier of security-oriented products and services for critical sites across various verticals.

As we see from the company's 5-year chart below, shares have been on an incessant rally since the early innings of 2024, with the stock up over 170% over the past year alone. The 5-year chart, however, as we see, is not as favourable (22% capital gain growth), but as mentioned, the chart encapsulates the larger entity discussed earlier.

What also must be pointed out is that two special dividends were paid in 2020 and 2021, amounts of $1.08 and $1.73 per share, respectively. These numbers were significant given that they made up a sizable percentage of Senstar's market cap at the time. When we evaluate stocks that release a sizable portion of their cash to shareholders at determined times, it is important to run our analysis on total returns and not solely the reported capital gain in the stock. What this means is that since our buy rating back in December 2020, the total return in this play amounts to an impressive 71%, irrespective of the 6% capital-gain loss in the stock during this time.

Stability In Q2 Earnings Report Required In Q3

Micro-cap companies many times fail to report stability in their financials, which leads to heightened volatility, the absence of a defined trend, which ultimately can lead to investor disinterest over time. This is understandable, given Senstar's market cap of approximately $110 million (lower on average revenue base), in that the financials can become lumpy (due to order and shipping dates not following an orderly routine).

However, in the last two quarters (Q2 earnings were reported on August 25, 2025), we have been pleased with the growth profile, margins, and strong diversification across multiple markets that we have seen from Senstar. Consolidated revenue grew by 16.2% to come in at $9.7 million, with both the corrections & energy verticals driving growth in the quarter. The higher growth resulted in a better gross margin (66.1%), demonstrating efficient economies of scale. The trend above should not be underestimated, given that the same metric in Q2 of fiscal 2024 came in a 62.65% from revenues of $8.3 million. Increasing gross profit is crucial for Senstar, as SG&A costs and R&D are likely to remain elevated to maintain its position at the forefront in a highly competitive industry.

Earnings Still Relatively Cheap After Excellent Year-To-Date

Although revenues from Canada as well as the Asia Pacific region declined in Q2 due to timing-related issues alluded to earlier, the EMEA region grew by 52% year over year, closely followed by the US market, which grew by 35% in the quarter. GAAP profit came in at $1.2 million, which means the second quarter was the fourth consecutive quarter where GAAP profit came in above the $1 million mark. This trend has positive ramifications both for Senstar's valuation and for keeping investor sentiment bullish, all things remaining equal.

Senstar's trailing GAAP multiple now comes in at 21.72, meaning it compares favourably with the sector median of 31. Moreover, the fact that Senstar has ample growth potential in the verticals it already serves, plus the possibility to leverage its excellent MultiSensor technology to noncritical infrastructure as the CEO outlines below, ample earnings on the income statement are important to ensure sustained investment can continue. Remember, Senstar carries zero debt on its balance sheet, so meaningful impediments to scaling the company remain limited at this juncture.

In addition, Senstar is actively working to broaden its addressable market by targeting the protection of critical points within noncritical infrastructure, such as hospitals, educational institutions and logistics facilities. Leveraging our unrivalled MultiSensor, we deliver unique performance by eliminating nuisance alarm rate, optimising total cost of ownership and significantly reducing installation and maintenance costs, unlocking opportunities in a much larger market segment.

Valuation

Following on from the keen GAAP earnings multiple alluded to earlier, Senstar's closing cash balance at the end of Q2 came in at $21.9 million, meaning the company is trading for approximately 5 times its cash position. Senstar's P/B and P/S come in at 2.63 and 2.91, well below the medians in this sector but ahead of the company's 5-year averages (1.39 & 1.36, respectively). However, we negate the notion that Senstar stock is overvalued based on historic multiples due to how the company has changed in recent years. To confirm this, Senstar's trailing growth and profitability trends remain light-years ahead of 5-year averages, explaining why valuation multiples have increased in recent years.

Risks As We Approach The Release Of Q3 Earnings

SNT is expected to announce its third-quarter earnings on 11/25/2025. Investors will be looking for more top-line growth supported by stability when it comes to bottom-line earnings. Senstar, as mentioned, is a micro-cap stock with below-average selling volume. This means, aggressive selling can easily break below support at any given time (especially around binary events such as earnings announcements) due to lower investor participation overall.

Furthermore, the lack of an established 'recurring revenue' model in Senstar's makeup means new locations will be needed to keep pushing the boat out with respect to revenue growth. Although we saw plenty of cross-selling among existing customers in Q2, you feel that if Senstar is not capable of bringing new customers onto its books continuously, then top-line growth may suffer as a result. Furthermore, with Senstar also aiming to branch out its services to non-critical infrastructure, this begs the question of whether management feels it can grow its market share aggressively enough within the critical infrastructure verticals it already serves.

Conclusion

To sum up, we still rate Senstar a 'Buy' based on its improving margins and profitability, strong top-line growth trends, and keen valuation. We recommend, however (given this company's micro-cap nature), that long exposure should be actively managed and position sizing should be kept in check. Let's see what Q3 earnings deliver. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: WisdomTree: Recent Price Weakness Is A Buy Opportunity
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I think WisdomTree (WT) is well positioned to grow its business over the long term, due to its focus on passive investing and its efforts to diversify its product offering toward new growth asset classes, such as digital assets. However, its share price has been on a downtrend over the past weeks, which can potentially be a buying opportunity for long-term investors. As can be seen in the next graph, WisdomTree has underperformed the overall stock market since my last article, as the market has penalized ‘growth’ stocks in recent weeks.

Despite that, the company’s fundamentals remain good, and WisdomTree has recently reported its quarterly earnings, which have maintained a positive operating momentum, showing that its recent share price weakness is not justified by potential company-specific issues.

Indeed, WisdomTree reported positive net flows during the third quarter of 2025 (Q3 2025) of about $2.2 billion, which, together with positive market moves, resulted in record assets under management (AuM) of more than $137 billion at the end of last September.

The company reported record AuMs both in the U.S. and Europe, supported by rising capital markets across equities and bonds, but also by new products. Indeed, WisdomTree launched a new ETF focused on defense companies in Europe some months ago, which now has more than $4 billion in AuMs, being an important contribution for higher AuMs in the region. During the first nine months of 2025, its net flows amounted to some $8.8 billion, of which $5.8 billion were raised in Europe, $2.5 billion in the U.S., and $500 million related to digital assets.

On top of organic growth, the company also closed during October the acquisition of Ceres Partners, a U.S.-based alternative asset manager that specializes in farmland investments. While its AuM is below $2 billion, this acquisition adds about $40 million in annual revenues, representing about 9% of WisdomTree’s revenues, and is also positive for higher business margins ahead.

To finance this acquisition, WisdomTree issued a $475 million convertible bond with maturity in 2030, bearing an interest rate of 4.625%. The company used proceeds from this issue to pay $275 million cash upfront for Ceres Partners but also used $37 million to repurchase a convertible bond with maturity in 2028 and about $90 million to repurchase its own shares to offset dilution from the issuance of the new convertible bond. As the acquisition only closed in October, WisdomTree had $660 million of cash in its balance sheet at the end of Q3 2025, but adjusted for the cash outflow for Ceres Partners, its cash position was around $385 million.

Its net debt adjusted for the cash outflow should be around $569 million, which means its net debt position more than doubled after this acquisition that was fully financed by debt. As the company’s EBITDA is expected to be around $225 million in 2026, this leads to a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of about 2.5x, which is significantly higher than before the Ceres Partners acquisition (around 1.5x leverage). This means WisdomTree now has a higher leverage position, and further large acquisitions are not very likely ahead, as WisdomTree is likely to retain cash flow generation to reduce balance sheet leverage over the coming years.

This also means that its dividend policy is not very likely to change in the near future, being quite likely that it will maintain its quarterly dividend unchanged at $0.03 per share ahead, like it has done since 2018. At its current share price, this leads to a dividend yield slightly above 1%, which is not particularly attractive to income-oriented investors.

Regarding its operating performance, its revenues were supported by higher average AuM and a slightly different product mix, even though its revenue yield of 38 basis points (bps) in Q3 2025 was slightly down compared to the same quarter of the previous year (revenue yield of 39 bps). Its revenues amounted to $125 million over the last quarter, representing an increase of 11% YoY, supported mainly by higher AuMs.

While its operating expenses also increased at double-digits in recent quarters, WisdomTree was able to report positive operating leverage leading to an operating income of nearly $46 million in Q3 2025, up 12% YoY. On the other hand, following the issue of the convertible bond due in 2030, its interest expense increased compared to the previous quarter, and the company also booked a loss of $13 million related to the repurchase of convertible notes, resulting in a net income of $19.7 million during the last quarter. Adjusted for one-off effects, its net income was $35 million in Q3 2025, representing annual growth of nearly 20%.

This shows that WisdomTree’s operating performance remains quite good and its business growth is well on track, a trend that is likely to continue in the near term as the company continues to make efforts to expand the product range and diversify its revenue stream.

For instance, WisdomTree has recently launched an ETF related to quantum computing stocks, which carries an expense ratio of 45 bps (higher than its revenue yield of 38 bps), aiming to track the performance of stocks in the quantum computing industry, such as Rigetti Computing (RGTI), Ionq (IONQ), or Quantum Computing (QUBT). As investors are increasingly interested in the potential of quantum computing, this is a product that allows exposure to a diversified basket of stocks in this investment theme, potentially being an important support for net flows in the short term.

Moreover, the company also made a recent strategic investment in AlphaBeta ETF Ltd, buying a minority stake in the business for a total amount of $2.5 million. While this is a relatively small investment, it allows WisdomTree to have access to AlphaBeta's technology and knowledge related to AI-based investing. This will help WisdomTree to use AI not only to operate more efficiently but also to use AI for its core business, namely in research, index design, and portfolio management parts of its operation.

Going forward, the company expects to maintain a growth path and report expanding business margins due to the integration of Ceres Partners, even though there have been some flow headwinds recently. Indeed, total AuMs amounted to $136 billion at the end of last October, representing a decline compared to the end of Q3 2025, justified by nearly $2.3 billion of net outflows during the month. These outflows were mainly related to fixed-income products and also to some profit-taking in gold-related products following gold’s strong return over the past few months.

Related to Q4 2025, WisdomTree’s guidance is to report a gross margin of 83% due to the integration of Ceres Partners, representing an increase of about 180 bps compared to the previous quarter. Given that Ceres Partners generates around $40 million of annual revenue and operating expenses are about $15 million, this means WisdomTree should report revenues of $135 million in Q4 and an operating profit closer to $50 million, up by almost 10% compared to the previous quarter. This is explained by Ceres Partners’ operating profit margin (62%) being much higher than WisdomTree’s on a stand-alone basis (around 36%), showing that Ceres’ integration has a positive immediate effect on business profitability.

Regarding its valuation, WisdomTree is currently trading at about 12.5x forward earnings, a much cheaper valuation compared to when I last analyzed it (it was trading at some 17x forward earnings at the time). This is also a lower valuation than compared to its historical average over the past five years (close to 17x earnings), showing that WisdomTree is currently cheap following some share price correction in recent weeks. This was a similar move compared to other ‘growth’ stocks in the financial industry, such as Tradeweb Markets (TW) that I’ve covered recently, as the market has become more concerned about some signs of growth slowdown in the industry over the past few weeks.

Conclusion

WisdomTree has reported a good operating performance in Q3 2025, reaching record AuMs, and closed the acquisition of Ceres Partners, being positive for growth and business margins ahead. Despite that, net outflows in October and general concerns about growth issues in the financial industry have resulted in a share price pullback, being a buying opportunity for long-term investors in my opinion, as the company’s fundamentals remain strong and WisdomTree’s growth strategy remains compelling.

data.title: Boston Omaha: 'Hold' Was The Right Choice; Entry Potential Is Here
data.description:
I covered Boston Omaha (BOC) about 8 months back, citing concerns for historical negative returns and internal turmoil creating longer-term uncertainty for the company, and as a result, making it a "HOLD" rather than a "BUY", despite fundamental appeal in some areas like billboards.

Boston Omaha remains a sub-$500M company operating in theoretically attractive operations, with good results, but the market has yet to pick up on its overall appeal. The returns since the last article, found here, look like this.

The company has underperformed the market, making the "HOLD" rating correct. Provided that we have an operationally positive result, this could make the company more interesting here. But BOC is, due to its size and exposure, a company that I do not focus on too frequently - updating during earnings updates and quarterlies. Also, as I have covered in my previous article, due to its tricky operating model resulting in negative earnings year after year (not expected to change in 2025 or 2026), evaluating it with typical or traditional methods is tricky. It means that in most cases, as I invest, there are more attractive companies around.

The company's earnings being negative has explanations (net other expenses, looking at financials, which are related to things like investments and operating cost increases, as well as some D&A), and while it might not offer a "fair picture" of the company, it is what investors tend to focus on.

In this article, I will be covering recent results and why they are tricky, even when considering the resilience of billboards and the like.

Boston Omaha - Even the resilience argument does not carry the company here

Some core issues for BOC, that I see, include a tapering-off rate of revenue growth. Current results and estimates do not guide for impressive forward growth, nor is it about to repeat the trip from 2017 to 2024, which saw the company increase its revenues 9-10x.

The positive is, on the other hand, that EBITDA and earnings are potentially expected to increase in a year or more, though due to aforementioned negatives, the net income is still expected to be in the negative.

Boston Omaha is not exactly a frequently covered business on Seeking Alpha. The last article was in April, almost 8 months ago. That means 2 full quarters have gone by without anyone touching upon the company's investment appeal, or lack thereof.

The positive thesis for Boston Omaha is typically centered around its broadband business as a growth driver, addressing needs in an area that is otherwise not well-covered by peers. It is also typically characterized as a longer-term value play, with a market cap that does not match its earnings potential going forward.

For the time being, color me unconvinced by the positives for the company.

I could write the company's stock price reaction off as unfavorable results in 2Q and 3Q, with continued net losses (even now, as of the latest report), as well as profitability issues, and the question of what exactly would make the company grow. The company has repeatedly missed analyst estimates for not only revenues, but also for earnings. The company's weakness when a multiple-sector company underperforms is starting to show here, with Sky Harbour being the most obvious example. Last period, the company saw an unrealized loss of $10M for the investment. The company (Sky Harbour) further has a book value of less than $100M, but a supposed value of over $170M, which creates potential valuation risk in case of debt/interest (which the company has), and because BOC is exposed to this, this is a risk for BOC as well.

The case for the company would be very different if its core operations—billboards, broadband, AM, and insurance—were profitable enough to generate positive earnings and offset the negatives in these investments. It is not, not as of the latest results either. I consider the market reaction to the company's negatives entirely fair, maybe even a little too much of a drop given everything.

BOC does not have a dividend. You, as the investor, get nothing except capital appreciation - and when the stock drops, you literally get nothing (and lose money). The company lacks a dividend; the company has a sub-$390M market capitalization as of the time of writing this article.

Arguments for investing in this company are, as I see it, few and far between. The thesis early in 2025E was cash flow increases and stability from core, with a focus on long-term value creation. The argument has often been "the market is not realizing its value".

Some of the positive arguments that can be made about the company are, as I see them, not to be disputed. Do I think the company's businesses, meaning advertising, broadband, and so on, will still exist a decade from now? I would say yes, they will. Do I think the company is in danger of immediate bankruptcy? My answer would be a firm "no". The company's balance sheet, despite everything, is relatively solid here. Less than 16% long-term debt to capital makes the business interesting from that perspective.

Less interesting is the 7-year 2-year based forecast accuracy of missing targets negatively 100% of the time, or the current forecasts, which put AEPS in negative territory not only for 2025E, but for 2026E as well. 3Q25, which was released yesterday, confirms this. Negative $0.08/share for EPS (GAAP), a significant miss once again, and a revenue miss as well.

At some future point, I have no doubt that the company will be attractive and a "BUY". But I do not, at this time, believe it to be the time to invest in Boston Omaha.

There is a reason I gave the company a PT of $11/share. We are still at $12+ today. So Boston Omaha has further to drop before I would be willing to "get in" here.

Let me run you through my assumptions again.

Valuation for Boston Omaha - Unattractive at this valuation

Because of the negative earnings and net results, we cannot use P/E for this company. Well, we could, but we would reach a negative 97x P/E for the company as of right now, and despite an expected 40% AEPS growth rate, the expectation is negative.

So let us avoid that.

What do we do with a company with negative earnings? We need to focus on metrics that are asset-based or forward-looking. We can use revenue multiples, book value, and other methods. Advanced analysts who work in the space may also try the VC valuation method (for venture capital), where an analyst would work backward from a future "exit" value - but that would be less relevant here.

Unfortunately, things do not get much easier with sales or revenue multiples. What I can clearly say is that since 2020, the company's revenue multiples and sales multiples are a lot less than they were - it is down from over 5-8x, which would be too high, to about 3-3.6x in terms of both. But whether this is in any way appealing will, of course, depend on what the company does with its revenues, with its cash.

Boston Omaha is no longer followed by analysts from S&P Global, as far as I can see. The last-updated price target is 3 months old, and details a low-end price target of $14/share as well as a higher-end of $22/share, implying an upside close to 100% if you believe that one.

The best method that is sort of my "fallback" here is what the company is worth per share in terms of book value. At this particular time, the total book value on a per-share basis, meaning the total balance sheet assets per the company's 31.45M shares, comes to $16.8/share. But before you jump for joy, remember that the company has done write-downs of its assets and taken hits for its investments.

Still, I view the book value as the relevant multiple here, properly discounted. I previously discounted book value at 35%, down to about $11.2, making it $11/share to account for all the investment risk in Sky Harbour, as I see the other segments (advertising, broadband) as cash-generative businesses with a good future, especially advertising and broadband.

There is some truth to the idea that the market has yet to "wake up" to what Boston Omaha offers. The problem is, I think, that it will be some time before it might be justified for the market to actually wake up.

As it stands, even holding your money in plain cash with zero interest would have been a better investment than buying Boston Omaha. That is not a favorable comparison. To those who argue that Sky Harbour could be a "gem," yes, it could. It could also be nothing. Based on the company's almost unfailing negative earnings, a forecast failure of 100%, and an LT/debt to capital of almost 75%, it is not my idea of a "safe investment" currently.

You might ask what you could buy instead, while waiting to see what happens with Boston Omaha?

I would go for Realty Income (O) as an option. 5.71% on cash monthly, A-rated and with proven stability and a current upside of double digits on a 20-year average AFFO basis. It is one of the few REITs I still hold actively and invest in.

But Boston Omaha? That is a company that I would currently avoid.

Risks & Considerations

Risks for Boston Omaha are, to me, very clear. The company is a smaller-cap business without any sort of yield, and with negative net earnings, impacted by continual D&A and investment losses. While the company's core business lines generate impressive safety and stability, for the past few years, the other businesses' losses and write-downs have impacted the valuation, despite the market at the time of writing being almost at a "high" or peak valuation. This makes arguing for an investment here very tricky.

The reason is that if the company has been reporting losses, and is continuing to expect to report losses (as it seems to be at this time), then why would the valuation turn around in the hypothetical situation that the market crashes? I view it as far more likely that the company will drop alongside the market. This means that even if you are bullish on Boston Omaha, you should consider investing when the company gets cheaper.

And that, dear readers, is what makes this company such a tricky play and a "HOLD" with a PT of $11 to me. I even considered lowering the target.

Thesis

  • Boston Omaha has many investors convinced of its fundamental value found in attractive subsidiaries and holdings, which are performing very well.
  • However, I believe this is a good example of where the market simply will not value a stock as "you want" due to management complications and lack of clarity. The company has been on a new journey since mid-2024, but I fail to see any near-term catalysts that could change the share price to anywhere positive, where I could make a good return, buffeted by good safety. This forecast, which I gave in early 2025, has turned out to be exactly the right way to handle this.
  • I, therefore, say that BOC is a "HOLD" and give the company a share price of $11/share, about 30% below the current lowest analyst share price out there (from S&P Global). This was the target in my last article, and it remains my target now - the recent results give me no reason to bump this.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically is not enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $11/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of November 2025.
